summary:
The Cishan culture, named after the site of Cishan in Hebei province of northern China and dated from approximately 6500 to 5000 BCE, was an early Neolithic culture of the North China Plain that provides some of the earliest evidence of millet cultivation in the world. Excavations at the Cishan site uncovered storage pits containing the remains of millet, with estimates of stored grain quantities suggesting a significant and organized agricultural economy even at this early date.

Cishan communities kept domesticated pigs and dogs, cultivated millet, and also exploited a wide range of wild resources. Their material culture includes simple pottery vessels and grinding stones for millet processing. The culture is closely related to the contemporary [[event:1425|Peiligang culture]] of adjacent Henan province and shares many features with it, suggesting these early millet-farming communities across the North China Plain formed part of a broader regional cultural complex.

The Cishan site's large millet storage pits are particularly significant for what they imply about food storage, surplus management, and possibly social organization. The ability to accumulate and store substantial grain reserves implies planning, organized labor, and some mechanism for managing stored resources — all features associated with increasing social complexity. Cishan thus provides important early evidence for the relationship between surplus grain agriculture and the development of more complex community organization in prehistoric China.
